questions.text: A supermarket sells its used refrigerant to another company. Before that refrigerant can legally change hands for reuse, it must be:

options.t: Reclaimed — reprocessed to AHRI 700 purity and chemically verified, typically by an EPA-certified reclaimer
Recycled — run through an on-site recycling machine with fresh filter-driers
options.why: Recycling only cleans refrigerant enough for reuse within the same owner's equipment. Crossing an ownership boundary is exactly what triggers the reclaim requirement.

options.t: Recovered — as long as it was removed with certified recovery equipment, it can be resold as-is
options.why: Recovery is just removal and storage — no processing at all. Recovered refrigerant cannot enter the market without reclamation.

options.t: Re-blended by any wholesaler to match the original composition specification
options.why: "Re-blending by a wholesaler" is not a Section 608 category. Purity restoration to AHRI 700 with lab verification is what the rule requires — that is reclamation.

questions.why: The three R's differ by destination: recover = remove/store; recycle = on-site cleanup for same-owner reuse; reclaim = AHRI 700 purity + chemical analysis, required before refrigerant changes ownership.

questions.text: You are recovering R-22 from a 300-lb commercial system using a recovery machine built in 2020. Per EPA, you must evacuate the appliance to:

options.t: 0 inches Hg (atmospheric) — that is the R-22 requirement
options.why: 0 in Hg applies to HCFC-22 appliances containing LESS than 200 lbs. At 300 lbs you are in the ≥200-lb row: 10 in Hg.
Microns belong to dehydration/evacuation before recharging — an industry practice, not an EPA recovery level. The recovery table is written in inches Hg.
15 in Hg is the deepest table value (very-high-pressure/other rows) — over-recall. The R-22 ≥200 lbs row with a post-1993 machine is 10 in Hg.

questions.why: EPA required-evacuation table: HCFC-22 appliance ≥200 lbs, recovery equipment manufactured after Nov 15 1993 → 10 in Hg vacuum. (<200 lbs → 0 in Hg.) Separate concept from the 500-micron dehydration pull.

questions.text: Which of these releases is actually LEGAL under the Section 608 venting prohibition?

options.t: The de minimis refrigerant that escapes while making a good-faith connection or disconnection during recovery
Venting R-410A, because it contains no chlorine and does not harm the ozone layer
options.why: The venting prohibition was extended to HFC substitutes back in 1995. Zero ODP does not make release legal.

options.t: Venting a small A2L charge, because its GWP is under the 700 threshold
options.why: The GWP-700 line is an equipment-manufacturing rule (Technology Transitions), not a venting exemption. Venting A2Ls is illegal.

options.t: Blowing refrigerant through a clogged filter-drier to clear debris from the line
options.why: Using system refrigerant as a "blow-out gas" is a knowing release — a classic exam distractor and a real fine in the field. Use recovered charge + nitrogen instead.

questions.why: Legal releases are a short, specific list: de minimis escape during good-faith recovery practices, nitrogen holding charges, and a few EPA-exempted substitutes in listed applications. Everything else stays in the system or the tank.

questions.text: When filling a recovery cylinder, you stop at 80% of its capacity. Why 80%?

options.t: To leave expansion room so rising temperature cannot hydrostatically over-pressurize the cylinder
To keep the cylinder light enough for one-person DOT-legal lifting
options.why: Ergonomics is not the rule's basis — liquid expansion is. A hydrostatically full cylinder can rupture with lethal force as temperature rises.

options.t: Because the last 20% of any cylinder is non-condensables by design
options.why: Non-condensables are a contamination issue diagnosed by pressure checks — nothing to do with the fill limit.

options.t: It is only a manufacturer recommendation; filling to 95% is acceptable when the tank is kept cool
options.why: The 80% fill limit is a hard safety rule (DOT/EPA exam material), not a soft recommendation — "keeping it cool" is not a control you can guarantee in a truck.

questions.why: Liquid refrigerant expands with temperature. The 80% limit preserves vapor space so the cylinder cannot go hydrostatically solid and rupture. Pair it with: DOT-approved cylinder, never mix refrigerants, transport upright.
questions.ref: EPA 608 Core cylinder safety; DOT 49 CFR
